preFix.title: Reducing packaging waste from online orders
preFix.subtitle: Consolidation, messaging retailers, and choosing platforms with less packaging
preFix.excerpt: The average UK online order arrives in packaging 4 to 5 times larger than needed, often with additional void fill. Consolidating orders, using retailer packaging preference tools, and choosing alternatives to fast-dispatch delivery reduces the packaging volume per order by 30 to 70%. This covers the practical changes that have the most effect.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP UK e-commerce packaging report 2022. Green Alliance online retail packaging research. Which? investigation into online retail packaging waste.

content.text: E-commerce packaging is sized for the worst-case item in a retailer's range and dispatched automatically. Most orders do not receive packaging sized for the actual contents. An individual order cannot change that system, but a set of choices at order time (when to order, which platform, what delivery speed) meaningfully changes the packaging volume that arrives.

Consolidate orders rather than ordering as each item is needed. Most retailers (including Amazon with its 'group my items' setting) can ship multiple items in one box when they originate from the same warehouse. Waiting 2 to 3 days to group items typically halves packaging volume compared to daily single-item dispatch.

Use retailer packaging preference settings where they exist. Amazon offers 'Frustration-Free Packaging' and 'No Rush Shipping with sustainability credit' for eligible items. John Lewis, Marks and Spencer, and Patagonia have all introduced reduced packaging options. Check the details at checkout.

Avoid express or next-day delivery when you do not need it. Express dispatch prioritises speed over consolidation, so items from the same retailer's distribution centre are sent separately. Standard delivery allows the warehouse to batch shipments.
Buy direct from manufacturers or specialist retailers whose item ranges are narrow. A specialist retailer dispatches fewer box sizes and wastes less
because their packaging is matched to their product dimensions.

Use click-and-collect for items that would arrive individually. A click-and-collect order may still be dispatched to a depot box, but the last-mile packaging (jiffy bags, polybags for clothing) is avoided.
What to do with packaging that arrives
Cardboard boxes and paper wadding: kerbside recycling in all UK councils. Flatten boxes before putting out.

Air pillows and polythene film: not accepted in kerbside recycling in most councils. Collect and return to a supermarket carrier bag drop (most major chains accept them) or local plastic film recycling point.

Polystyrene chips: not recyclable in most council schemes. Some Ryman stationery shops and specialist packaging suppliers will accept polystyrene for reuse. Alternatively, compost in very small quantities (breaks down extremely slowly) or use as drainage layer in plant pots.
